revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''春日曼荼羅'''（かすがまんだら）とは、[[平安時代]]末期から[[江戸時代]]にかけて作成された[[春日大社]]およびそれに...'
revision.text:
'''春日曼荼羅'''（かすがまんだら）とは、[[平安時代]]末期から[[江戸時代]]にかけて作成された[[春日大社]]およびそれに関連する事象を描いた[[神道]][[曼荼羅図]]のこと。
[[藤原氏]]による[[氏神]]・春日大社に対する崇敬と[[神仏習合]]の影響を受けて、特に[[中世]]（[[鎌倉時代]]・[[室町時代]]）に盛んに制作された。主なものとして、春日大社の[[神鹿]]を描いた「鹿曼荼羅」、春日大社の[[本地仏]]を描いた「本地曼荼羅」、[[春日山 (奈良県)|春日山]]を背景として春日大社及び若宮とその本地仏である五尊を描いた「宮曼荼羅」、[[氏寺]]・興福寺を合わせて描いた「社寺曼荼羅」、[[鹿島神宮]]の[[明神]]が春日大社に[[影向]]（ようごう）した模様を描いた「鹿島立神影図」など様々な種類があり、春日大社への崇敬が[[奈良]]を中心として藤原氏以外にも広まるにつれて「垂迹神曼荼羅」「本迹曼荼羅」「地蔵曼荼羅」「浄土曼荼羅」など新たな派生が生まれた。
== 参考文献 == *景山春樹「春日曼荼羅」（『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *川村知行「春日曼荼羅」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *宮島新一「春日曼荼羅」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かすかまんたら}} [[Category:曼荼羅]] [[Category:神仏習合]]

'''春日曼荼羅'''（かすがまんだら）とは、[[平安時代]]末期から[[江戸時代]]にかけて作成された[[春日大社]]およびそれに関連する事象を描いた[[神道]][[曼荼羅図]]のこと。
[[藤原氏]]による[[氏神]]・春日大社に対する崇敬と[[神仏習合]]の影響を受けて、特に[[中世]]（[[鎌倉時代]]・[[室町時代]]）に盛んに制作された。主なものとして、春日大社の[[神鹿]]を描いた「鹿曼荼羅」<ref>「鹿曼荼羅」は[[近衛基通]]が夢想によって感得したと伝えられている（『平安時代史事典』「春日曼荼羅」項目）。</ref>、春日大社の[[本地仏]]を描いた「本地曼荼羅」、[[春日山 (奈良県)|春日山]]を背景として春日大社及び若宮とその本地仏である五尊を描いた「宮曼荼羅」、[[氏寺]]・興福寺を合わせて描いた「社寺曼荼羅」、[[鹿島神宮]]の[[明神]]（[[タケミカヅチ]]）が春日大社に[[影向]]（ようごう）した模様を描いた「鹿島立神影図」など様々な種類があり、春日大社への崇敬が[[奈良]]を中心として藤原氏以外にも広まるにつれて「垂迹神曼荼羅」「本迹曼荼羅」「地蔵曼荼羅」「浄土曼荼羅」など新たな派生が生まれた。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *景山春樹「春日曼荼羅」（『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *川村知行「春日曼荼羅」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *宮島新一「春日曼荼羅」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6） *宇野茂樹「春日曼荼羅」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かすかまんたら}} [[Category:曼荼羅]] [[Category:神仏習合]]

[[ファイル:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|本地曼荼羅]] '''春日曼荼羅'''（かすがまんだら）とは、[[平安時代]]末期から[[江戸時代]]にかけて作成された[[春日大社]]およびそれに関連する事象を描いた[[神道]][[曼荼羅図]]のこと。
[[藤原氏]]による[[氏神]]・春日大社に対する崇敬と[[神仏習合]]の影響を受けて、特に[[中世]]（[[鎌倉時代]]・[[室町時代]]）に盛んに制作された。主なものとして、春日大社の[[神鹿]]を描いた「鹿曼荼羅」<ref>「鹿曼荼羅」は[[近衛基通]]が夢想によって感得したと伝えられている（『平安時代史事典』「春日曼荼羅」項目）。</ref>、春日大社の[[本地仏]]を描いた「本地曼荼羅」、[[春日山 (奈良県)|春日山]]を背景として春日大社及び若宮とその本地仏である五尊を描いた「宮曼荼羅」、[[氏寺]]・興福寺を合わせて描いた「社寺曼荼羅」、[[鹿島神宮]]の[[明神]]（[[タケミカヅチ]]）が春日大社に[[影向]]（ようごう）した模様を描いた「鹿島立神影図」など様々な種類があり、春日大社への崇敬が[[奈良]]を中心として藤原氏以外にも広まるにつれて「垂迹神曼荼羅」「本迹曼荼羅」「地蔵曼荼羅」「浄土曼荼羅」など新たな派生が生まれた。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *景山春樹「春日曼荼羅」（『国史大辞典 3』（吉川弘文館、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *川村知行「春日曼荼羅」（『日本史大事典 2』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *宮島新一「春日曼荼羅」（『日本歴史大事典 1』（小学館、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6） *宇野茂樹「春日曼荼羅」（『平安時代史事典』（角川書店、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かすかまんたら}} [[Category:曼荼羅]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:春日神社|*]]

revision.comment: Created page with "[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] The **Kasuga Mandala** (春日曼荼羅, Kasuga Mandara) refers to a series of [[Shinto]] [[mandala]] paintings depicting the [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and related themes, created from the late [[Heian period]] through the [[Edo period]]. These mandalas were heavily influenced by the [[Fujwara clan]]'s veneration of Kasuga Taisha, their ancestral shrine, as well as the concept of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of..."
revision.text:
[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] The **Kasuga Mandala** (春日曼荼羅, Kasuga Mandara) refers to a series of [[Shinto]] [[mandala]] paintings depicting the [[Kasuga Taisha]] Shrine and related themes, created from the late [[Heian period]] through the [[Edo period]].
These mandalas were heavily influenced by the [[Fujwara clan]]'s veneration of Kasuga Taisha, their ancestral shrine, as well as the concept of [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[medieval period]] (the [[Kamakura period]] and [[Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: - **Deer Mandala** (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> - **Honji Mandala** (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[honji-butsu]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. - **Shrine Mandala** (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of [[Kasugayama]]. - **Temple-Shrine Mandala** (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[Kōfuku-ji]]. - **Kashima Deity Procession Painting** (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[Takemikazuchi]] of [[Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in [[Nara]], additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
## References <references/>
## Further Reading - Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *National History Encyclopedia, Vol. 3* (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. - Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *Encyclopedia of Japanese History, Vol. 2* (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. - Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *Japanese History Encyclopedia, Vol. 1* (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. - Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* (Kadokawa Shoten, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
{{DEFAULTSORT:Kasuga Mandara}} [[Category:Mandala]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Kasuga Shrines|+Kasuga Mandara]]

As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

[[Category:Mandala]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Kasuga Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kasuga Mandalas-qq]] [[Category:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kasuga Mandalas]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[ja:春日曼荼羅]]

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. revision.text:
[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. == Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:春日曼荼羅]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kasuga Mandalas]][[Category:qq]] {{Kasuga Faith}}

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).

The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.

Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

revision.text:
[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

revision.text:
[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=draft}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神|12=draft}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|12=draft}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通|12=draft}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|12=draft}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=draft}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺|12=draft}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=draft}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|12=draft}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=draft}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
<!--Kasuga Mandalas-->

<!--Kasuga Mandalas--> {{Kasuga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mandala]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Kasuga Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kasuga Mandalas]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]] [[ja:春日曼荼羅]] [[en:Kasuga Mandalas]]

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=draft|12=draft}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft|12=draft}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神|12=draft|12=draft}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|12=draft|12=draft}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|12=draft|12=draft}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=draft|12=draft}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|12=draft|12=draft}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft|12=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft|12=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=draft|12=draft}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
<!--Kasuga Mandalas--> {{Kasuga Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Mandala]] [[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Kasuga Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]]

revision.comment: 9 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:春日曼荼羅 up to rev 93069876
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|本地曼荼羅]] '''春日曼荼羅'''（かすがまんだら）とは、[[平安時代]]末期から[[江戸時代]]にかけて作成された[[春日大社]]およびそれに関連する事象を描いた[[神道]][[曼荼羅図]]のこと。
[[藤原氏]]による[[氏神]]・春日大社に対する崇敬と[[神仏習合]]の影響を受けて、特に[[中世]]（[[鎌倉時代]]・[[室町時代]]）に盛んに制作された。主なものとして、春日大社の[[神鹿]]を描いた「鹿曼荼羅」<ref>「鹿曼荼羅」は[[近衛基通]]が夢想によって感得したと伝えられている（『平安時代史事典』「春日曼荼羅」項目）。</ref>、春日大社の[[本地仏]]を描いた「本地曼荼羅」、[[春日山 (奈良県)|春日山]]を背景として春日大社及び若宮とその本地仏である五尊を描いた「宮曼荼羅」、[[氏寺]]・興福寺を合わせて描いた「社寺曼荼羅」、[[鹿島神宮]]の[[明神]]（[[タケミカヅチ]]）が春日大社に[[影向]]（ようごう）した模様を描いた「鹿島立神影図」など様々な種類があり、春日大社への崇敬が[[奈良]]を中心として藤原氏以外にも広まるにつれて「垂迹神曼荼羅」「本迹曼荼羅」「地蔵曼荼羅」「浄土曼荼羅」など新たな派生が生まれた。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *景山春樹「春日曼荼羅」（『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 3』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *川村知行「春日曼荼羅」（『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]]、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *宮島新一「春日曼荼羅」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]]、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6） *宇野茂樹「春日曼荼羅」（『[[平安時代史事典]]』（[[角川書店]]、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かすかまんたら}} [[Category:曼荼羅]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:春日神社|+かすかまんたら]]

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|本地曼荼羅]] '''春日曼荼羅'''（かすがまんだら）とは、[[平安時代]]末期から[[江戸時代]]にかけて作成された[[春日大社]]およびそれに関連する事象を描いた[[神道]][[曼荼羅図]]のこと。
[[藤原氏]]による[[氏神]]・春日大社に対する崇敬と[[神仏習合]]の影響を受けて、特に[[中世]]（[[鎌倉時代]]・[[室町時代]]）に盛んに制作された。主なものとして、春日大社の[[神鹿]]を描いた「鹿曼荼羅」<ref>「鹿曼荼羅」は[[近衛基通]]が夢想によって感得したと伝えられている（『平安時代史事典』「春日曼荼羅」項目）。</ref>、春日大社の[[本地仏]]を描いた「本地曼荼羅」、[[春日山 (奈良県)|春日山]]を背景として春日大社及び若宮とその本地仏である五尊を描いた「宮曼荼羅」、[[氏寺]]・興福寺を合わせて描いた「社寺曼荼羅」、[[鹿島神宮]]の[[明神]]（[[タケミカヅチ]]）が春日大社に[[影向]]（ようごう）した模様を描いた「鹿島立神影図」など様々な種類があり、春日大社への崇敬が[[奈良]]を中心として藤原氏以外にも広まるにつれて「垂迹神曼荼羅」「本迹曼荼羅」「地蔵曼荼羅」「浄土曼荼羅」など新たな派生が生まれた。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参考文献 == *景山春樹「春日曼荼羅」（『[[国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|国史大辞典]] 3』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1983年） ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6） *川村知行「春日曼荼羅」（『[[日本史大事典]] 2』（[[平凡社]]、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4） *宮島新一「春日曼荼羅」（『[[日本歴史大事典]] 1』（[[小学館]]、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6） *宇野茂樹「春日曼荼羅」（『[[平安時代史事典]]』（[[角川書店]]、1994年） ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:かすかまんたら}} [[Category:曼荼羅]] [[Category:神仏習合]] [[Category:春日神社|+かすかまんたら]]

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|1=Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|lt=Honji Buddha|2=ja|3=本地垂迹|4=fr|5=Honji suijaku|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
{{Kasuga Faith}}
{{translated page|ja|春日曼荼羅|version=93069876|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} paintings depicting {{ill|Kasuga Taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and related themes, created from the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} through the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The mandalas were influenced by the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|ja|藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'s veneration of their {{ill|ujigami|ja|氏神|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|1=Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|lt=Honji Buddha|2=ja|3=本地垂迹|4=fr|5=Honji suijaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|National History Encyclopedia|ja|国史大辞典 (昭和時代)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|ja|小学館|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|ja|角川書店|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|medieval period|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/medieval period|qq=}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|ja|鎌倉時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura period|qq=}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|ja|近衛基通|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konoe Motomichi|qq=}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|1=Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|lt=Honji Buddha|2=ja|3=本地垂迹|4=fr|5=Honji suijaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji-butsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|qq=}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|qq=}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|ja|興福寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfuku-ji|qq=}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|ja|タケミカヅチ|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|ja|鹿島神宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kashima Shrine|qq=}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

revision.text:
[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mandala|qq=}} paintings depicting [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] and related themes, created from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|1=Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|lt=Honji Buddha|2=ja|3=本地垂迹|4=fr|5=Honji suijaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji-butsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|qq=}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|qq=}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|qq=}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|qq=}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|1=Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|lt=Honji Buddha|2=ja|3=本地垂迹|4=fr|5=Honji suijaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji-butsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|qq=}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|qq=}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|qq=}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|qq=}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
{{Kasuga Faith}}
{{translated page|ja|春日曼荼羅|version=93069876|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Shinbutsu-shūgō]] [[Category:Kasuga Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Kasuga Mandalas]] }}
[[sn:Kasuga Mandalas]] [[ja:春日曼荼羅]] [[en:Kasuga Mandalas]]
<!--Kasuga Mandalas--> [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] [[Category:Mandalas]]

[[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mandala|qq=}} paintings depicting [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] and related themes, created from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|honji-butsu|ja|本地仏|1=Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|lt=Honji Buddha|2=ja|3=本地垂迹|4=fr|5=Honji suijaku|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/honji-butsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Honji_suijaku#Honji Buddha|qq=}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|qq=}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|qq=}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|qq=}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
{{Kasuga Faith}}

Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|qq=|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.

== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|qq=|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|qq=|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|qq=|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
