<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 曼荼羅}} paintings depicting [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] and related themes, created from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 曼荼羅}} paintings depicting [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] and related themes, created from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
{{Kasuga Faith}}
{{translated page|ja|春日曼荼羅|version=93069876|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 6]]

This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.

== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

[[Category:Mandalas]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Kasuga Mandala}} {{wikidata link|Q11513629}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 曼荼羅}} paintings depicting [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] and related themes, created from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga Mandala]][[ja:春日曼荼羅]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513629]] Wikidata name: Kasuga mandala [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kasugamandala detail.jpg|thumb|Honji Mandala]] '''Kasuga Mandala''' (春日曼荼羅) refers to a series of [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] {{ill|mandala|ja|曼荼羅図|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mandala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 曼荼羅}} paintings depicting [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] and related themes, created from the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] through the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].
The mandalas were influenced by the [[:en:Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara clan]]'s veneration of their [[:en:Ujigami|ujigami]] Kasuga Taisha, as well as by the concept of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). Their production flourished particularly during the [[:en:Middle Ages|medieval period]] (the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] and [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]]).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by [[:en:Konoe Motomichi|Konoe Motomichi]].<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the [[:en:Honji suijaku|Honji Buddha]] (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, [[:en:Kōfuku-ji|Kōfuku-ji]]. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity [[:en:Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi]] of [[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Shrine]] to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *[[:en:Kokushi Daijiten|National History Encyclopedia]] Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ([[:en:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]], 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ([[:en:Kadokawa Shoten|Kadokawa Shoten]], 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
{{Kasuga Faith}}

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|en|Konoe Motomichi|WD=Q367538|lt=Konoe Motomichi}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|en|Kokushi Daijiten|WD=Q11420603|lt=National History Encyclopedia}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|WD=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.

This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|en|Konoe Motomichi|WD=Q367538|lt=Konoe Motomichi|1=Konoe Motomichi}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha|1=Honji suijaku}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.

Their production flourished particularly during the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=medieval period|1=Middle Ages}} (the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}).
Prominent examples include: * '''Deer Mandala''' (鹿曼荼羅, Shika Mandara): Featuring the sacred deer of Kasuga Taisha. This type is said to have originated from a vision experienced by {{ill|Konoe Motomichi|en|Konoe Motomichi|WD=Q367538|lt=Konoe Motomichi|1=Konoe Motomichi}}.<ref>According to the *Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia* entry on "Kasuga Mandala."</ref> * '''Honji Mandala''' (本地曼荼羅, Honji Mandara): Depicting the {{ill|Honji suijaku|en|Honji suijaku|WD=Q3080781|lt=Honji Buddha|1=Honji suijaku}} (original Buddhist counterparts) of the deities enshrined at Kasuga Taisha. * '''Shrine Mandala''' (宮曼荼羅, Miya Mandara): Featuring Kasuga Taisha and Wakamiya Shrine, with the Five Honji Buddhas as the main focus, set against the background of {{ill|Kasugayama|ja|春日山 (奈良県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasugayama|1=Mount Kasuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11513602}}. * '''Temple-Shrine Mandala''' (社寺曼荼羅, Jinja Tera Mandara): Incorporating both Kasuga Taisha and its associated temple, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|WD=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}}. * '''Kashima Deity Procession Painting''' (鹿島立神影図, Kashima-Tachi Shinkage-zu): Depicting the divine descent of the deity {{ill|Takemikazuchi|en|Takemikazuchi|WD=Q1502690|WD=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi|1=Takemikazuchi}} of {{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Shrine|1=Kashima Jingū}} to Kasuga Taisha.
As reverence for Kasuga Taisha expanded beyond the Fujiwara clan to a broader audience, particularly in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara|qq=}}, additional derivatives like the "Suijaku Mandala," "Honjaku Mandala," "Jizō Mandala," and "Pure Land Mandala" emerged.
== References ==
<references/>
== Further Reading == * Haruki Kageyama, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|en|Kokushi Daijiten|WD=Q11420603|lt=National History Encyclopedia|1=Kokushi Daijiten}} Vol. 3* ({{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1983), ISBN 978-4-642-00503-6. * Tomoyuki Kawamura, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja|日本史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Encyclopedia of Japanese History|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Vol. 2* ({{ill|Heibonsha|en|Heibonsha|WD=Q11482108|lt=Heibonsha|1=Heibonsha}}, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13102-4. * Shinichi Miyajima, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja|日本歴史大事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese History Encyclopedia|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135524488|1=Nihon Rekishi Daijiten}} Vol. 1* ({{ill|Shogakukan|en|Shogakukan|WD=Q785697|lt=Shogakukan|1=Shogakukan}}, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523001-6. * Shigeki Uno, "Kasuga Mandara," *{{ill|Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|ja|平安時代史事典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Period Historical Encyclopedia|1=Heian Jidaishi Jiten|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11482399}}* ({{ill|Kadokawa Shoten|en|Kadokawa Shoten|WD=Q1054765|lt=Kadokawa Shoten|1=Kadokawa Shoten}}, 1994), ISBN 978-4-04-031700-7.
